title: Der Gevatter Tod
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 44. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The forty-fourth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 44), a stark parable of Death as the one impartial power. A poor man with thirteen children rejects God and the Devil as godfather — the one favours the rich, the other cheats mankind — and chooses Death, who takes rich and poor alike without distinction. Death makes the boy a famous physician who can read a fatal omen (Death at the patient’s head means recovery, at the feet means death) and heal with a secret herb; but the doctor twice cheats Death by turning the patient around, and Death repays the trick by letting his godson’s own candle of life gutter out. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as mußte, wußte, Noth, gieng, ward, Reichthum, Armuth, vertheilt, Pathe, Eigenthum, gerathen and thut.

sentences.t: Es hatte ein armer Mann zwölf Kinder und mußte Tag und Nacht arbeiten damit er ihnen nur Brot geben konnte.
sentences.l: There was a poor man who had twelve children and had to work day and night just so that he could give them bread.
sentences.i: A poor man had twelve children, and he had to work day and night merely to keep them in bread.
sentences.n: “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste (preterite of müssen, ‘had to’). “damit ... geben konnte” is a purpose clause, ‘so that he could give’; the finite verb goes to the end.

sentences.t: Als nun das dreizehnte zur Welt kam, wußte er sich in seiner Noth nicht zu helfen, lief hinaus auf die große Landstraße und wollte den ersten, der ihm begegnete, zu Gevatter bitten.
sentences.l: When now the thirteenth came into the world, he did not know how to help himself in his distress, ran out onto the great highway, and meant to ask the first who met him to stand godfather.
sentences.i: Now when the thirteenth came into the world, he was at his wits’ end in his distress; he ran out onto the great highway and meant to ask the first man he met to be godfather.
sentences.n: “zur Welt kam” = came into the world, i.e. was born. “sich nicht zu helfen wissen” is an idiom, ‘to be at a loss, not know what to do.’ “Noth” is the 1857 spelling of Not (distress, need). “zu Gevatter bitten” = to ask (someone) to act as godfather.

sentences.t: Der erste der ihm begegnete, das war der liebe Gott, der wußte schon was er auf dem Herzen hatte, und sprach zu ihm „armer Mann, du dauerst mich, ich will dein Kind aus der Taufe heben, will für es sorgen und es glücklich machen auf Erden.“
sentences.l: The first who met him, that was the dear God, who already knew what he had on his heart, and said to him, “poor man, you move me to pity, I will lift your child out of the font, will care for it and make it happy on earth.”
sentences.i: The first he met was the dear Lord, who already knew what was on his mind, and said to him, “Poor man, I pity you; I will be your child’s godfather, will care for it and make it happy on earth.”
sentences.n: “du dauerst mich” is idiomatic: ‘you move me to pity, I feel sorry for you.’ “aus der Taufe heben” (literally ‘to lift out of the font’) is the set phrase for acting as godparent at a baptism. “auf Erden” is an old dative, ‘on earth.’

sentences.t: Der Mann sprach „wer bist du?“
sentences.l: The man said, “who are you?”

sentences.t: „Ich bin der liebe Gott.“
sentences.l: “I am the dear God.”
sentences.i: “der liebe Gott” — ‘the dear Lord,’ the ordinary German name for God in such tales.

sentences.t: „So begehr ich dich nicht zu Gevatter,“ sagte der Mann, „du giebst dem Reichen und lässest den Armen hungern.“
sentences.l: “Then I do not desire you for godfather,” said the man, “you give to the rich and let the poor go hungry.”
sentences.i: “Then I don’t want you for a godfather,” said the man, “you give to the rich and let the poor starve.”
sentences.n: “begehr” is apocopated “begehre” (I desire). “giebst” is the 1857 spelling of gibst (you give). “dem Reichen … den Armen” are nominalized adjectives, ‘the rich (man) … the poor (man).’

sentences.t: Das sprach der Mann, weil er nicht wußte wie weislich Gott Reichthum und Armuth vertheilt.
sentences.l: That the man said because he did not know how wisely God apportions wealth and poverty.
sentences.i: The man said this because he did not know how wisely God apportions riches and want.
sentences.n: “weislich” is an archaic adverb, ‘wisely, prudently.’ “Reichthum,” “Armuth” and “vertheilt” are 1857 spellings of Reichtum, Armut and verteilt (the -th- and -uth are old orthography).

sentences.t: Also wendete er sich von dem Herrn und gieng weiter.
sentences.l: So he turned himself away from the Lord and went further.
sentences.i: So he turned away from the Lord and walked on.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (went) — you meet this -ie- spelling throughout the edition (gieng, fieng, hieng).

sentences.t: Da trat der Teufel zu ihm und sprach „was suchst du? willst du mich zum Pathen deines Kindes nehmen, so will ich ihm Gold die Hülle und Fülle und alle Lust der Welt dazu geben.“
sentences.l: Then the Devil stepped up to him and said, “what do you seek? if you will take me as godfather of your child, then I will give it gold in plenty and all the pleasure of the world besides.”
sentences.i: Then the Devil came up to him and said, “What are you after? Take me for your child’s godfather, and I will give it gold in abundance and all the pleasures of the world besides.”
sentences.n: “Pathe” is the 1857 spelling of Pate (godfather). “die Hülle und Fülle” is a fixed rhyming phrase, ‘more than enough, in abundance.’ The “willst du … , so will ich …” frame is ‘if you will …, then I will …’.

sentences.t: Der Mann fragte „wer bist du?“
sentences.l: The man asked, “who are you?”

sentences.t: „Ich bin der Teufel.“
sentences.l: “I am the Devil.”

sentences.t: „So begehr ich dich nicht zum Gevatter,“ sprach der Mann, „du betrügst und verführst die Menschen.“
sentences.l: “Then I do not desire you for godfather,” said the man, “you deceive and lead people astray.”
sentences.i: “Then I don’t want you for a godfather,” said the man, “you cheat and corrupt mankind.”
sentences.n: Note the small variation: “zu Gevatter” with God, “zum Gevatter” with the Devil — Grimm is not rigidly consistent, and both mean ‘for godfather.’

sentences.t: Er gieng weiter, da kam der dürrbeinige Tod auf ihn zugeschritten und sprach „nimm mich zu Gevatter.“
sentences.l: He went further; then the lean-legged Death came striding toward him and said, “take me for godfather.”
sentences.i: He walked on, and then gaunt-legged Death came striding up to him and said, “Take me for godfather.”
sentences.n: “dürrbeinig” = lean-legged, spindle-shanked — Death pictured as a skeleton. “kam zugeschritten” = came striding along; zuschreiten is separable (‘to stride toward’).

sentences.t: Der Mann fragte „wer bist du?“
sentences.l: The man asked, “who are you?”

sentences.t: „Ich bin der Tod, der alle gleich macht.“
sentences.l: “I am Death, who makes all equal.”
sentences.i: The relative clause “der alle gleich macht” — ‘who makes everyone equal’ — is Death’s calling card: he is the great leveller.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Mann „du bist der rechte, du holst den Reichen wie den Armen ohne Unterschied, du sollst mein Gevattersmann sein.“
sentences.l: Then the man said, “you are the right one, you fetch the rich as (well as) the poor without distinction, you shall be my godfather.”
sentences.i: Then the man said, “You are the right one; you fetch the rich and the poor alike, without distinction — you shall be my child’s godfather.”
sentences.n: This is the moral hinge of the tale: the man rejects God and the Devil, but chooses Death precisely because Death is impartial (“ohne Unterschied,” without distinction). “Gevattersmann” is an expanded form of Gevatter, ‘godfather.’

sentences.t: Der Tod antwortete „ich will dein Kind reich und berühmt machen, denn wer mich zum Freunde hat, dem kanns nicht fehlen.“
sentences.l: Death answered, “I will make your child rich and famous, for whoever has me for a friend, for him it cannot go wrong.”
sentences.i: Death answered, “I will make your child rich and famous, for whoever has me for a friend can never fail.”
sentences.n: “wer …, dem …” is a correlative construction: ‘whoever …, for that one …’. “kanns” = kann es, with the clitic es; “dem kanns nicht fehlen” = ‘nothing can go amiss for him.’

sentences.t: Der Mann sprach „künftigen Sonntag ist die Taufe, da stelle dich zu rechter Zeit ein.“
sentences.l: The man said, “next Sunday is the baptism, so present yourself there at the right time.”
sentences.i: The man said, “Next Sunday is the christening; be there in good time.”
sentences.n: “künftigen Sonntag” = next/coming Sunday (accusative of time). “stelle dich … ein” is the separable verb sich einstellen, ‘to present oneself, turn up.’

sentences.t: Der Tod erschien wie er versprochen hatte, und stand ganz ordentlich Gevatter.
sentences.l: Death appeared as he had promised, and stood godfather quite properly.
sentences.i: Death appeared as he had promised, and stood godfather in due and proper form.
sentences.n: “Gevatter stehen” is the set phrase ‘to stand as godfather.’ “ganz ordentlich” = quite properly, in due form.

sentences.t: Als der Knabe zu Jahren gekommen war, trat zu einer Zeit der Pathe ein und hieß ihn mitgehen.
sentences.l: When the boy had come to years, the godfather stepped in one day and bade him go along.
sentences.i: When the boy had grown up, his godfather came in one day and bade him come along.
sentences.n: “zu Jahren gekommen” is an idiom, ‘come of age, grown up.’ “hieß ihn mitgehen” — heißen here means ‘to bid, command’; ‘bade him go along.’

sentences.t: Er führte ihn hinaus in den Wald, zeigte ihm ein Kraut, das da wuchs, und sprach „jetzt sollst du dein Pathengeschenk empfangen.
sentences.l: He led him out into the forest, showed him a herb that grew there, and said, “now you shall receive your godfather’s gift.
sentences.i: He led him out into the forest, showed him a herb growing there, and said, “Now you shall have your christening gift.
sentences.n: “Pathengeschenk” = the gift a godfather traditionally gives — here no trinket but a supernatural power.

sentences.t: Ich mache dich zu einem berühmten Arzt.
sentences.l: I make you into a famous physician.
sentences.i: I will make you a famous physician.

sentences.t: Wenn du zu einem Kranken gerufen wirst, so will ich dir jedesmal erscheinen: steh ich zu Häupten des Kranken, so kannst du keck sprechen, du wolltest ihn wieder gesund machen, und gibst du ihm dann von jenem Kraut ein, so wird er genesen; steh ich aber zu Füßen des Kranken, so ist er mein, und du mußt sagen alle Hilfe sei umsonst und kein Arzt in der Welt könne ihn retten.
sentences.l: When you are called to a sick person, then I will appear to you every time: if I stand at the head of the sick one, you may speak boldly that you would make him well again, and if you then give him a dose of that herb, then he will recover; but if I stand at the feet of the sick one, then he is mine, and you must say all help is in vain and no physician in the world could save him.
sentences.i: Whenever you are called to a sick person, I will appear to you each time: if I stand at the sick man’s head, you may boldly declare that you will make him well again, and if you then give him a dose of that herb, he will recover; but if I stand at his feet, he is mine, and you must say that all help is in vain and no physician in the world could save him.
sentences.n: This is the omen at the heart of the tale — Death at the head means recovery, Death at the feet means death. “zu Häupten / zu Füßen” are old dative-plural phrases, ‘at the head / at the feet.’ “ein…geben” is separable: “gibst du ihm ein” = give him a dose (of medicine). “sei” and “könne” are subjunctive I of reported speech — the doctor is to report Death’s verdict, not assert his own.

sentences.t: Aber hüte dich daß du das Kraut nicht gegen meinen Willen gebrauchst, es könnte dir schlimm ergehen.“
sentences.l: But beware that you do not use the herb against my will, it could go ill for you.”
sentences.i: But take care not to use the herb against my will, or it may go badly for you.”
sentences.n: “hüte dich” = beware, take care (sich hüten). “es könnte dir schlimm ergehen” — ergehen with the dative, ‘things could turn out badly for you’; a foreshadowing.

sentences.t: Es dauerte nicht lange, so war der Jüngling der berühmteste Arzt auf der ganzen Welt.
sentences.l: It did not last long, and the youth was the most famous physician in all the world.
sentences.i: Before long the young man was the most famous physician in all the world.
sentences.n: “Es dauerte nicht lange, so …” is a common Grimm frame, ‘it was not long before ….’ “berühmteste” is the superlative of berühmt.

sentences.t: „Er braucht nur den Kranken anzusehen, so weiß er schon wie es steht, ob er wieder gesund wird, oder ob er sterben muß,“ so hieß es von ihm, und weit und breit kamen die Leute herbei, holten ihn zu den Kranken und gaben ihm so viel Gold, daß er bald ein reicher Mann war.
sentences.l: “He needs only to look at the sick person, and he already knows how it stands, whether he will get well again or whether he must die,” so it was said of him, and far and wide the people came, fetched him to the sick and gave him so much gold that he was soon a rich man.
sentences.i: “He need only look at the patient, and he knows at once how things stand — whether the man will recover or must die,” so it was said of him; and people came from far and wide, fetched him to the sick, and gave him so much gold that he was soon a rich man.
sentences.n: “anzusehen” is ansehen (‘to look at’) with the zu of the infinitive splitting the separable prefix, an-zu-sehen. “wie es steht” = how matters stand. “so hieß es von ihm” = so it was said of him.

sentences.t: Nun trug es sich zu, daß der König erkrankte: der Arzt ward berufen und sollte sagen ob Genesung möglich wäre.
sentences.l: Now it came about that the king fell ill: the physician was summoned and was to say whether recovery were possible.
sentences.i: Now it happened that the king fell ill; the physician was sent for and was to say whether recovery was possible.
sentences.n: “es trug sich zu” = it came to pass (sich zutragen). “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden, here forming the passive ‘was summoned.’ “wäre” is subjunctive of the reported question.

sentences.t: Wie er aber zu dem Bette trat, so stand der Tod zu den Füßen des Kranken, und da war für ihn kein Kraut mehr gewachsen.
sentences.l: But as he stepped to the bed, Death stood at the feet of the sick one, and there no herb had grown for him any more.
sentences.i: But when he came to the bed, Death was standing at the sick man’s feet, and for the king no herb would avail.
sentences.n: “Bette” has the old dative -e (modern Bett). “da war kein Kraut mehr gewachsen” is a vivid idiom: ‘no herb had grown any more for him,’ i.e. there was no remedy — the omen at the feet means death.

sentences.t: „Wenn ich doch einmal den Tod überlisten könnte,“ dachte der Arzt, „er wirds freilich übel nehmen, aber da ich sein Pathe bin, so drückt er wohl ein Auge zu: ich wills wagen.“
sentences.l: “If only I could outwit Death for once,” thought the physician, “he will surely take it amiss, but since I am his godson, he will no doubt turn a blind eye: I will risk it.”
sentences.i: “If only I could outwit Death for once,” thought the physician; “he’ll take it amiss, of course, but as I am his godson he’ll surely turn a blind eye — I’ll chance it.”
sentences.n: “überlisten” = to outwit. “übel nehmen” = to take amiss, resent. “ein Auge zudrücken” is an idiom, ‘to turn a blind eye’ (drücken … zu, separable). “wills / wirds” carry the clitic es (will es / wird es). Note that Pathe here means the godson (the doctor), not the godfather.

sentences.t: Er faßte also den Kranken und legte ihn verkehrt, so daß der Tod zu Häupten desselben zu stehen kam.
sentences.l: So he took hold of the sick man and laid him the wrong way round, so that Death came to stand at the head of him.
sentences.i: So he took hold of the sick man and turned him round, so that Death came to stand at his head instead.
sentences.n: “verkehrt legen” = to lay the wrong way round, reverse. “desselben” = ‘of the same one,’ i.e. of him. The doctor’s trick: reverse the patient so Death’s deadly position at the feet becomes the healing one at the head.

sentences.t: Dann gab er ihm von dem Kraute ein, und der König erholte sich und ward wieder gesund.
sentences.l: Then he gave him a dose of the herb, and the king recovered himself and became well again.
sentences.i: Then he gave him a dose of the herb, and the king recovered and was well again.
sentences.n: “Kraute” has the old dative -e. “eingeben” (gab … ein) = to administer, give a dose. “sich erholen” = to recover.

sentences.t: Der Tod aber kam zu dem Arzte, machte ein böses und finsteres Gesicht, drohte mit dem Finger und sagte „du hast mich hinter das Licht geführt: diesmal will ich dirs nachsehen, weil du mein Pathe bist, aber wagst du das noch einmal, so geht dirs an den Kragen, und ich nehme dich selbst mit fort.“
sentences.l: But Death came to the physician, made an evil and dark face, threatened with his finger and said, “you have led me behind the light: this time I will overlook it for you, because you are my godson, but if you dare that once more, then it goes for your throat, and I take you yourself away with me.”
sentences.i: But Death came to the physician, put on a black and forbidding look, wagged his finger and said, “You have hoodwinked me. This time I will let it pass, because you are my godson; but dare it once more and it will cost you your neck — I shall carry you off myself.”
sentences.n: “Arzte” is the old dative of Arzt. “jemanden hinter das Licht führen” is an idiom, ‘to deceive, hoodwink someone.’ “etwas nachsehen” = to overlook, let pass. “es geht dir an den Kragen” = ‘it is a matter of your neck,’ your life is at stake. “dirs / drohte” — dirs = dir es.

sentences.t: Bald hernach verfiel die Tochter des Königs in eine schwere Krankheit.
sentences.l: Soon after, the daughter of the king fell into a grave illness.
sentences.i: Soon afterwards the king’s daughter fell gravely ill.
sentences.n: “hernach” = afterwards. “verfiel in eine Krankheit” — verfallen in, ‘to fall into (an illness).’

sentences.t: Sie war sein einziges Kind, er weinte Tag und Nacht, daß ihm die Augen erblindeten, und ließ bekannt machen wer sie vom Tode errettete, der sollte ihr Gemahl werden und die Krone erben.
sentences.l: She was his only child; he wept day and night, so that his eyes went blind, and had it made known: whoever rescued her from death, he should become her husband and inherit the crown.
sentences.i: She was his only child; he wept day and night until his eyes went blind, and had it proclaimed that whoever saved her from death should become her husband and inherit the crown.
sentences.n: “daß ihm die Augen erblindeten” — a result clause, ‘so that his eyes went blind’; “ihm” is a dative of the person affected. “Tode” has the old dative -e. “wer …, der …” again the correlative ‘whoever …, he ….’ “erben” = to inherit.

sentences.t: Der Arzt, als er zu dem Bette der Kranken kam, erblickte den Tod zu ihren Füßen.
sentences.l: The physician, when he came to the bed of the sick one, caught sight of Death at her feet.
sentences.i: When the physician came to the sick girl’s bed, he caught sight of Death at her feet.
sentences.n: “erblickte” = caught sight of. Death at the feet again means she is doomed — the doctor is about to break his promise a second time.

sentences.t: Er hätte sich der Warnung seines Pathen erinnern sollen, aber die große Schönheit der Königstochter und das Glück ihr Gemahl zu werden bethörten ihn so, daß er alle Gedanken in den Wind schlug.
sentences.l: He ought to have remembered the warning of his godfather, but the great beauty of the king’s daughter and the fortune of becoming her husband so beguiled him that he flung all thought to the wind.
sentences.i: He should have remembered his godfather’s warning, but the great beauty of the princess and the prospect of becoming her husband so beguiled him that he cast all caution to the wind.
sentences.n: “hätte … erinnern sollen” is the past subjunctive of obligation, ‘ought to have remembered’; sich erinnern takes the genitive (der Warnung). “bethörten” is the 1857 spelling of betörten (beguiled, infatuated). “in den Wind schlagen” is an idiom, ‘to throw to the wind, disregard.’

sentences.t: Er sah nicht daß der Tod ihm zornige Blicke zuwarf, die Hand in die Höhe hob und mit der dürren Faust drohte; er hob die Kranke auf, und legte ihr Haupt dahin, wo die Füße gelegen hatten.
sentences.l: He did not see that Death cast angry looks at him, raised his hand into the air and threatened with his withered fist; he lifted the sick girl up, and laid her head there where the feet had lain.
sentences.i: He did not see that Death cast angry looks at him, raised his hand aloft and shook his bony fist; he lifted the sick girl up and laid her head where her feet had lain.
sentences.n: “Blicke zuwerfen” = to cast glances at (zuwarf, separable zu-). “in die Höhe heben” = to raise aloft. “dürre Faust” — the withered/bony fist of the skeleton. He repeats the head/feet trick, reversing her body.

sentences.t: Dann gab er ihr das Kraut ein, und alsbald rötheten sich ihre Wangen, und das Leben regte sich von neuem.
sentences.l: Then he gave her the herb, and at once her cheeks reddened, and life stirred itself anew.
sentences.i: Then he gave her the herb, and at once her cheeks flushed red, and life stirred in her once more.
sentences.n: “alsbald” = at once, straightway. “rötheten sich” — sich röthen (1857 spelling of röten), ‘to grow red’; “von neuem” = anew, once again.

sentences.t: Der Tod, als er sich zum zweitenmal um sein Eigenthum betrogen sah, gieng mit langen Schritten auf den Arzt zu und sprach „es ist aus mit dir und die Reihe kommt nun an dich,“ packte ihn mit seiner eiskalten Hand so hart, daß er nicht widerstehen konnte, und führte ihn in eine unterirdische Höhle.
sentences.l: Death, when he saw himself cheated of his property for the second time, went with long strides toward the physician and said, “it is over with you, and the turn now comes to you,” seized him with his ice-cold hand so hard that he could not resist, and led him into an underground cave.
sentences.i: When Death saw himself cheated of his own for the second time, he strode up to the physician and said, “It is all over with you, and now it is your turn,” seized him with his ice-cold hand so hard that he could not resist, and led him into an underground cavern.
sentences.n: “Eigenthum” is the 1857 spelling of Eigentum (property) — the dead are Death’s rightful property. “die Reihe kommt an dich” = ‘the turn comes to you, it is your turn.’ “auf … zugehen” (gieng … zu) = to walk up to.

sentences.t: Da sah er wie tausend und tausend Lichter in unübersehbaren Reihen brannten, einige groß, andere halbgroß, andere klein.
sentences.l: There he saw how thousand upon thousand of lights burned in endless rows, some large, others half-grown, others small.
sentences.i: There he saw thousands upon thousands of lights burning in endless rows, some large, some middling, some small.
sentences.n: “unübersehbar” = impossible to take in at a glance, endless. “halbgroß” = half-grown, of middling size — these are the candles of life, sized by how much life remains.

sentences.t: Jeden Augenblick verloschen einige, und andere brannten wieder auf, also daß die Flämmchen in beständigem Wechsel hin und her zu hüpfen schienen.
sentences.l: Every moment some went out, and others flared up again, so that the little flames seemed to hop hither and thither in constant alternation.
sentences.i: Every moment some went out and others flared up, so that the little flames seemed to dart to and fro in constant change.
sentences.n: “verloschen” (verlöschen) = went out; “brannten … auf” (aufbrennen) = flared up, both marking deaths and births. “Flämmchen” is the diminutive of Flamme. “hin und her” = to and fro.

sentences.t: „Siehst du,“ sprach der Tod, „das sind die Lebenslichter der Menschen.
sentences.l: “Do you see,” said Death, “these are the life-lights of men.
sentences.i: “Do you see,” said Death, “these are the candles of life of mankind.
sentences.n: “Lebenslicht” — literally ‘life-light,’ the candle whose burning is a person’s span of life; the central image of the tale’s ending.

sentences.t: Die großen gehören Kindern, die halbgroßen Eheleuten in ihren besten Jahren, die kleinen gehören Greisen.
sentences.l: The large ones belong to children, the half-grown ones to married folk in their best years, the small ones belong to old men.
sentences.i: The large ones belong to children, the middling ones to married people in their prime, the small ones to the aged.
sentences.n: “gehören” takes the dative (Kindern, Eheleuten, Greisen). “in ihren besten Jahren” = in their prime. The long candle is youth, the stub is old age.

sentences.t: Doch auch Kinder und junge Leute haben oft nur ein kleines Lichtchen.“
sentences.l: Yet children and young people too often have only a small little light.”
sentences.i: Yet children and young folk too often have only a tiny candle.”
sentences.n: “Lichtchen” is the diminutive of Licht — a tiny flame, a short life; a grim reminder that youth is no guarantee.

sentences.t: „Zeige mir mein Lebenslicht“ sagte der Arzt und meinte es wäre noch recht groß.
sentences.l: “Show me my life-light,” said the physician, and thought it would still be quite large.
sentences.i: “Show me my candle of life,” said the physician, thinking it would still be quite tall.
sentences.n: “meinte es wäre …” — meinen (to think, suppose) with subjunctive of reported thought, ‘thought it was ….’ Dramatic irony: he is sure of a long life.

sentences.t: Der Tod deutete auf ein kleines Endchen, das eben auszugehen drohte und sagte „siehst du, da ist es.“
sentences.l: Death pointed to a small little stub that was just threatening to go out, and said, “do you see, there it is.”
sentences.i: Death pointed to a small stub that was just about to go out and said, “Do you see — there it is.”
sentences.n: “deutete auf” = pointed at (deuten auf). “Endchen” is the diminutive of Ende, ‘a little stub.’ “auszugehen drohte” — ausgehen (to go out) with drohen (‘threatened to,’ i.e. was on the point of).

sentences.t: „Ach, lieber Pathe,“ sagte der erschrockene Arzt, „zündet mir ein neues an, thut mirs zu Liebe, damit ich meines Lebens genießen kann, König werde und Gemahl der schönen Königstochter.“
sentences.l: “Ah, dear godfather,” said the frightened physician, “light me a new one, do it for my sake, so that I may enjoy my life, become king and husband of the fair king’s daughter.”
sentences.i: “Ah, dear godfather,” said the terrified physician, “light me a new one — do it for my sake, that I may enjoy my life, become king and husband of the fair princess.”
sentences.n: The doctor now addresses Death as “Pathe” (godfather) and uses the polite plural (zündet, thut). “etwas zu Liebe thun” (1857 spelling; = zuliebe tun) = to do something for someone’s sake. “meines Lebens genießen” — genießen with the genitive, ‘to enjoy (of) my life.’

sentences.t: „Ich kann nicht,“ antwortete der Tod, „erst muß eins verlöschen, eh ein neues anbrennt.“
sentences.l: “I cannot,” answered Death, “first one must go out before a new one catches fire.”
sentences.i: “I cannot,” answered Death; “one must first go out before a new one can be lit.”
sentences.n: “verlöschen” = to go out; “anbrennen” = to catch fire, begin burning. “eh” is a short form of ehe (before). Death states the iron law: no new light without an old one dying.

sentences.t: „So setzt das alte auf ein neues, das gleich fortbrennt wenn jenes zu Ende ist,“ bat der Arzt.
sentences.l: “Then set the old one upon a new one, that burns on at once when that one is at an end,” begged the physician.
sentences.i: “Then set the old one on top of a new one, so that it burns straight on when the old is spent,” begged the physician.
sentences.n: “fortbrennen” = to burn on, keep burning (separable fort-). “jenes” = ‘that one,’ the old stub. The doctor bargains for a splice from old candle to new.

sentences.t: Der Tod stellte sich als ob er seinen Wunsch erfüllen wollte, langte ein frisches großes Licht herbei: aber weil er sich rächen wollte versah ers beim Umstecken absichtlich, und das Stückchen fiel um und verlosch.
sentences.l: Death made as if he would fulfil his wish, reached over a fresh large light: but because he wished to avenge himself, he bungled it at the re-setting on purpose, and the little piece fell over and went out.
sentences.i: Death made as though he would grant his wish and reached down a fresh, tall candle; but because he meant to take his revenge, he deliberately made a slip in the transfer, and the little stub toppled over and went out.
sentences.n: “sich stellen als ob” = to pretend that. “herbeilangen” = to reach over, fetch. “sich versehen” here means ‘to make a slip, err’; “versah ers” = versah er es. “Umstecken” = the re-plugging/transferring of the candle. “absichtlich” = deliberately — Death’s calculated revenge.

sentences.t: Alsbald sank der Arzt zu Boden, und war nun selbst in die Hand des Todes gerathen.
sentences.l: At once the physician sank to the ground, and was now himself fallen into the hand of Death.
sentences.i: At once the physician sank to the ground, and had now himself fallen into the hand of Death.
sentences.n: “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten; “in die Hand … gerathen” = to fall into the hand/power of. The trickster is at last taken by the one he cheated twice.
